coredata.dc:title: Highly selective fluorescent probe for benzidine determination using carbon dots fabricated from amoxicillin and sodium carbonate
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Menghua
Li, Shuangying
Li, Zhi
Li, Yanhui
Zhang, Yue
Niu, Jingwen
Nie, Linchun
Chen, Chunmao
Zhou, Qingxiang
coredata.dc:description: Amoxicillin is one of the most important semi-synthetic penicillin broad-spectrum β-lactam antibiotics and has achieved many applications. There are few reports on its application for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs). Present work explored the possibility of amoxicillin converted into CDs with sodium carbonate used as the co-reactant employing facile hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity to benzidine and could be explored as an effective fluorescence probe for monitoring benzidine. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched based on the principle of static quenching due to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between CD and benzidine. Within the range of 0.2–2 μM and 2–16 μM, CDs possessed good linear characteristics, high sensitivity, and a low detection limit of 0.08 μM. The results demonstrated that the fluorescence probe possessed the merits of simplicity, rapidity, low cost, short analytical time, high selectivity and sensitivity, and good stability. According to the verification results from the real water samples, the fluorescence probe had high reliability and feasibility, and could be employed for monitoring benzidine in waters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence probe
Amoxicillin
Benzidine
Static quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization for the determination of benzidine
Effect of the concentration of CDs
Fluorescence mechanism for the detection of benzidine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs at the excitation wavelength of 255–285 nm (B) The fluorescence intensity of CDs in the pH range of 1–14. (C) Fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 0%-25% NaCl concentration. (D) Study on the stability of the CDs. (E) XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, S2p, and Na1s of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs at 303 nm with addition of 0.5 μM benzidine before and after (Inset: CDs under sunlight and ultraviolet light (top), CDs in the absence and presence of benzidine under sunlight and ultraviolet light (bottom)), (B) Effect of the concentration of CDs, (C) Effect of pH value, (D) The Effect of incubation time. (Initial experimental parameters: CD concentration, 60 μg mL−1; pH, 6.5; reaction time, 0 min. Other parameters remained unchanged and the parameters were optimized. The optimal value is used in the subsequent optimization process).
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with benzidine concentration in the range of 0–16 μM, (B) The linear relationship between F0-F and benzidine from 0.2 to 2 μM (C) and 2–16 μM (D).
The selectivity of the fluorescent sensor for sensing benzidine.

(A) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs, benzidine and CDs-benzidine system, (B) UV–Vis spectrum of benzidine, excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of the CDs, (C) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs + benzidine.
Schematic diagram of the CDs preparation process and quenching effect.
Comparison of different methods with proposed sensor for the analysis of benzidine.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: HPLC, High performance liquid chromatography; HPLC-ECD, High performance liquid chromatography coupled to electrochemical detection; SERS, Surface enhanced Raman spectrometry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination results of benzidine in blank and spiked real water samples.

head.ce:title: Highly selective fluorescent probe for benzidine determination using carbon dots fabricated from amoxicillin and sodium carbonate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amoxicillin is one of the most important semi-synthetic penicillin broad-spectrum β-lactam antibiotics and has achieved many applications. There are few reports on its application for synthesizing carbon dots (CDs). Present work explored the possibility of amoxicillin converted into CDs with sodium carbonate used as the co-reactant employing facile hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity to benzidine and could be explored as an effective fluorescence probe for monitoring benzidine. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched based on the principle of static quenching due to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between CD and benzidine. Within the range of 0.2–2 μM and 2–16 μM, CDs possessed good linear characteristics, high sensitivity, and a low detection limit of 0.08 μM. The results demonstrated that the fluorescence probe possessed the merits of simplicity, rapidity, low cost, short analytical time, high selectivity and sensitivity, and good stability. According to the verification results from the real water samples, the fluorescence probe had high reliability and feasibility, and could be employed for monitoring benzidine in waters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Primary aromatic amines (PAAs) are extensively applied in chemical industry, polymer rubber, textile, medicine and other industries. For decades, they have been employed directly or as synthetic intermediates in various production processes, such as pesticides, textiles and azo dyestuffs, plastics, drugs and cosmetics [1]. Aromatic amines are highly toxic and may cause cancer. Many commercial products based on PAAs can be used as food contact materials. PAAs are hazardous in potential and may cause cancer and other toxic effects [2]. Benzidine is a typical PAA, which is common and mainly utilized for producing textiles, paper, printing presses, leather, drugs and foods [3]. Benzidine can cause environmental pollution and affect human health by industrial wastewater discharge. As benzidine has strong toxicity and carcinogenicity, the International Institute of cancer (IARC) has listed benzidine as a class I carcinogen [4]. Therefore, it is of utmost significance to establish appropriate methods for selective and sensitive analysis of benzidine in environment.

At present, various methods, including liquid chromatography, chromatography-mass spectrometry, colorimetry, surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) and ion chromatography, have been successfully developed for the determination of benzidine [5–11]. Although these methods earned high sensitivity, most of them usually required complex and expensive equipment. Among these technologies, fluorescence detection technique is simple, fast and sensitive for benzidine detection due to its merits such as simple operation, rapid reaction, short analysis time, high sensitivity and low detection limit. Generally, semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes are often-used fluorescent reagents for developing fluorescence sensors to detect various targets [12–14]. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) occurred as a new shining star in fluorescence analytical field because of their good merits including good biocompatibility, highly fluorescent stability, excellent water solubility, easy synthesis, and various source availability [15]. It has absorbed much attention from the researchers and obtained many applications including sensors [16,17], biological imaging [18], photocatalysis [19,20], and drug delivery [21,22]. There are mainly two methods to prepare CDs, namely “top-down” and “bottom-up” [23–25]. The top-down method is to decompose carbon materials into CDs [26], including ultrasonic synthesis [27], laser ablation [28], and electrochemical oxidation [29], etc. The bottom-up method widely used because of its simple operation, low cost, environmental protection and low cost, which include hydrothermal method [30], microwave method [31]and high temperature calcination method [32], etc. Kumari and coworkers transformed plastic waste into high luminous carbon dots (CDs) with a quantum yield of 59% through high-temperature calcination. The formed CDs have a strong fluorescence emission peak at 660 nm, an absorption peak at 270 nm, and have high selectivity for the ion of toxic pollutant sulfite (SO3 2-) [33]. Vigeata and coworkers prepared five different types of bright blue hair CQDs from different fruit parts of muskmelon by high-temperature calcination. The prepared CQDs possessed average particle size of about 5–10 nm, high quantum yield, high solubility, low toxicity and solvent polarity dependent absorption and emission characteristics. The developed CQDs showed higher stability over a period of 4 months [34]. Hydrothermal method is a facile, cheap, environment-friendly and widely utilized method, and the method uses Teflon autoclave to synthesize CDs by adding different precursors and adjusting reaction temperature and time [35]. Chen and coworkers synthesized nitrogen doped carbon dots using black fungus as raw material through hydrothermal method. N-CDs are spherical with an average particle size of 2.85 nm. The optimal excitation wavelength and emission wavelength are 324 nm and 400 nm, respectively [36]. Ni's group used chitosan as the main raw material to synthesize chitosan based solid carbon dots (C-SS-CDs) using one-step hydrothermal method. The photoluminescence quantum yield of C-SS-CDs in acid solution was 40%, which had good resistance to photobleaching and temperature cycle response, and can be used to detect Fe3+ ions in acidic environment [37].

Up to date, according to the principle of fluorescence quenching or enhancement, various CDs derived from different precursors have been applied for the determination of organic and inorganic pollutants. Cheng and coworkers synthesized orange emitting N, S-doped carbon dots by a hydrothermal method with basic fuchsin and sulfosalicylic acid as raw materials. On the basis of inner filtration effect (IFE), N, S-doped carbon dots can be used for effective measurement of aureomycin and quercetin [38]. Fang’s group fabricated copper doped CDs (Cu-CDs) with CuCl2·2H2O and ethylenediamine as the precursors by one-step hydrothermal method. The fluorescence intensity of the obtained Cu-CDs was significantly declined because of the existence of p-nitrophenol, and so a highly selective and sensitive p-nitrophenol fluorescence sensing platform was established based on IFE. The platform had been successfully utilized to quantitatively analyze p-nitrophenol in water samples, and had broad application prospect [39]. There is no report on the detection of benzidine by fluorescent probe based on CDs. Antibiotics are an important kind of pharmaceuticals and achieved many applications, few researches on the preparation and application of carbon dots derived from them have been found. Amoxicillin and sodium carbonate are rich in elemental composition, low cost and easily available, and are good precursors of CDs.

The goal of present work is to prepare new carbon dots with amoxicillin as carbon precursor by simple hydrothermal method, and explore its utilization for monitoring benzidine. The quenching mechanism between CDs and benzidine was discussed. The related parameters were examined. Scheme 1 demonstrates the preparation process and fluorescence quenching principle of CDs.

Amoxicillin (95%), sodium carbonate (95%), benzidine (95%), 4-n-butyl aniline (97%), p-methylaniline (99%), 4-methoxyaniline (99%), p-chloroaniline (99%) and aniline were provided by Aladdin Industry Co., Ltd. Other reagents and instruments used for characterization and determination in the supporting information.

The preparation procedure of CDs is as follows: 2.22 g amoxicillin and 0.12 g sodium carbonate were mixed completely in 15 mL ultra-pure water. The obtained solution, after ultrasonic treatment for 15 min, was transferred to the Teflon autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 10 h. The obtained brownish yellow solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane to dislodge impurities. CDs were purified for one day in a dialysis bag with the specification of MCWO:1000 Da, and then stored in the refrigerator. Finally, 2.0 mg mL−1 CDs solution was prepared with purified water.

For benzidine detection, 30 μL of 2.0 mg mL−1 CDs solution and a certain amount of benzidine solution were put into a centrifuge tube, and the mixed solution was diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water, and transferred to a quartz cuvette and measured on an FL7000 fluorescence spectrometer. The excitation wavelength and the emission wavelength were set at 275 nm and 303 nm, respectively. While the slit widths were all set at 5 nm. Aniline, 4-n-butyl aniline, 4-methoxyaniline, p-methylaniline and p-chloroaniline were selected to evaluate the selectivity for benzidine.

In order to assess the feasibility of practical application, four real water samples were collected from Beijing Jingmi diversion canal, Shahe, Anhui Nanhu Park and Shanghai Pearl River, respectively. The real water samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane to dislodge impurities.

According to the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CDs in Fig. S1A, it can be found that CDs were evenly dispersed and were appropriately spherical. Fig. S1A (inset) exhibits the particle size distribution of the prepared CDs. By measuring 40 random nanoparticles, the average diameter of CDs was 1.4 nm. The functional groups on the surface of CDs were revealed by FTIR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S1B. The peak at 3436 cm−1 belongs to N-H/O-H tensile vibration, and the peak at 1210 cm−1 belongs to O-H bending vibration. The peaks at 1760 cm−1,1648 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC and C-N [40], respectively. Therefore, all FTIR signals confirmed the existence of amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of the CDs, which helps to enhance its hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous solution [41].

To observe the fluorescent characteristics of the prepared carbon dots, 30 μL CDs solution (2 mg mL−1) was added to a centrifuge tube (2 mL), then diluted to 1.0 mL with ultrapure water. According to the result of pre-examination, the excitation and emission wavelengths were fixed at 275 nm and 303 nm, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. S2A, there is a small absorption peak at 225 nm and a broad absorption peak at 335 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum. The absorption peak at 225 nm is related to π-π* transition of CC bond, and the absorption peak at 335 nm corresponds to the n- π * transition of CO bond [42]. Fig. S2B was a 3D diagram of CDs. It can be seen that the maximum emission wavelength of the CDs occurred at 303 nm when the excitation wavelength was set to 225 nm and 275 nm. When the excitation wavelength was fixed at 265 nm and 350 nm, the maximum emission wavelength was appeared at 425 nm. As the fluorescence intensity of CDs excited at 265 nm was low, the fluorescence intensity excited at 225 nm was unstable, and benzidine itself has fluorescence under 350 nm excitation, excitation wavelength at 275 nm and emission wavelength at 303 nm were selected for subsequent experiments. As shown in Fig. 1 A, at the optimum excitation wavelength of 275 nm, the emission wavelength of CD was acquired at 303 nm. At the excitation wavelength of 255 to 285 nm, the shift of the fluorescence emission peak at 303 nm was almost neglected, which indicated that CDs were independent from excitation [43].

In order to study the optical stability of CDs, we investigated the effects of pH, ionic strength and storage time on the optical property of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1B, the fluorescence intensity changes of CD at pH 1.0–5.0 and 8–14 can be ignored. At pH 5.0–6.5 and 6.5–8, the change trend of fluorescence intensity is opposite, and the fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum at pH 6.5. Ionic strength is also a crucial factor that might affect the fluorescence emission of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1C, when the NaCl concentration gradually increased from 0% to 25%, the fluorescence intensity increased slightly, indicating that as-obtained CDs possessed excellent optical stability. Fig. 1D demonstrates that the fluorescent value of CDs occurred negligible changes within three months, indicating that the CDs owned excellent photo-stability. The elemental composition of CDs was further analyzed with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1E, CDs contain five elements: C, N, O, S, and Na. The peaks of C1s at 284 and 288 eV are C-C and CO, respectively. The peak of N1s at 399.0 eV is C-N-C. The two peaks of the O1s spectrum at 531 and 535 eV belong to CO and C-O, respectively [44,45]. The peaks of S2p at 164 and 168 eV are attributed to the C-S-C and SO [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for the determination of benzidine
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the sensing performance of CDs for benzidine, some important influencing factors were optimized, including CDs concentration, incubation time and pH value. In optimizing the parameters for sensing benzidine, the concentration of benzidine was fixed at 5 μM. ΔF(F0 − F) represents the fluorescence quenching value of CDs, where F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of CDs without benzidine, and F represents the fluorescence intensity of CDs with benzidine (Fig. 2 A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the concentration of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, CDs have a reabsorption effect at high concentrations, so the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots will decrease with large increase of concentration [47]. The effect of CDs concentration on fluorescence quenching effect was optimized in the range of 30–80 µg mL−1. As shown in Fig. 2B, with the increase of CD concentration, the fluorescence quenching value gradually increased, reaching the maximum at 60 µg mL−1, and then gradually decreased. Therefore, the CDs concentration of 60 μg mL−1 was optioned for subsequent experiments.

The fluorescence quenching of CD was caused by the formation of hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl groups on the surface of CD under strong acidic conditions. The fluorescence properties of CD were affected by the electron transition on surface functional groups of CDs under basic conditions. Due to the deprotonation of recognition sites, the quenching efficiency may be reduced under strong alkali conditions [48]. Therefore, it is much vital to select the appropriate pH to achieve the optimal quenching effect. Fig. 2C shows the effect of pH value on the fluorescence signal. ΔF value rapidly increased to the peak value ranged from pH 5.5 to pH 6.5, and then rapidly declined with continuous increase of pH value. As hydrogen ions can react with the amino groups on benzidine in strong acidic environment, which will reduce the interaction between CDs and benzidine, and further reduce quenching. Under strong alkali environment, hydroxyl ions will react with carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs, which weakens the interaction between CDs and benzidine and decreases the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Therefore, the solution pH was adjusted to pH 6.5 for subsequent experiments.

Incubation time is a crucial factor in the establishment of a fast and sensitive sensor. As shown in Fig. 2D, the ΔF was nearly unchanged within 0–10 min after adding benzidine to the CDs solution. The result showed that the reaction between CDs and benzidine was rapid and stable. Therefore, 2 min was selected as the best incubation time. It should be noted the response time of the CDs was only 2 min, which was faster than other probes reported in the literatures [49,50]. These results indicated that the constructed probe could rapidly detect benzidine in complex water environment, and had broad application prospect with merits of easy operation, rapidness, stability and convenience.

Under the above optimum conditions, the relationship between the quenching effect of CDs and benzidine concentration was explored. As shown in Fig. 3A, as the concentration of benzidine increased from 0 to 16 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 303 nm gradually decreased. In Fig. 3B, a good linear response with a correlation coefficient of 0.9932 was found over the benzidine concentration range of 0.2–2 μM. As shown in Fig. 3 C, another good linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of 0.9948 was achieved in the benzidine concentration range of 2–16 μM. The limit of detection (LOD) of benzidine was 0.08 μM. Zhao’s group utilized europium nitrate hexahydrate, 1,4-naphthalenedicarboxylic acid and 2-fluorobenzoic acid to prepare a new micro-sized lanthanide metal organic framework with octahedral morphology by solvothermal method. This new MOF significantly increased the luminescence efficiency of Eu3+ owing to antenna effect in which the new MOF structure markedly enhanced the energy transfer from 1,4-naphthalenedicarboxylic acid to Eu3+. The obtained MOF could be utilized to effectively analyze benzidine in ethanol solution because of the luminescence quenching effect from Eu3+ coordination with OH groups from the oxidized amino groups of benzidine. However, this developed method was used to determine benzidine in ethanol, which limited the application of this method [51]. Wang and co-workers prepared a novel multifunctional Fe3O4-Au nanocomposite with high solubility in water by a facile layer-by-layer technique. The mercapto silica was coated on Fe3O4 nanoclusters, and then the gold nanoparticles were stably modified on them by the formation of Au-S bonds. On the basis of the magnetic separability of Fe3O4 nanoclusters and the SERS activity of modified gold nanoparticles, an easy to operate, quick and sensitive determination method for benzidine was developed, and the LOD was about 0.9 μM [52]. As shown in Table 1 , the fluorescent probe had a low detection limit and wide linear range compared with other detection methods. Meanwhile, the probe had the advantages of simple synthesis, easy operation, fast response and high sensitivity. It provided a rapid and straightforward process for detecting benzidine.

Selectivity is an extremely feature parameter as a fluorescence probe is constructed for detecting benzidine. In this experiment, aniline, 4-n-butyl aniline, 4-methoxyaniline, p-methylaniline and p-chloroaniline were included for investigating the selectivity of the proposed probe. Firstly, 0.5 μM benzidine, 5.0 μM aniline, 5.0 μM 4-n-butyl aniline, 5.0 μM 4-methoxyaniline, 5.0 μM p-methylaniline and 5.0 μM p-chloroaniline were mixed with CDs solution respectively to observe the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs. Fig. 4 exhibits that ΔF had obvious change with addition of benzidine (black bar), and ΔF was very small with separate addition of other aromatic amine analogues. In addition, 0.5 μM benzidine and 5.0 μM aniline analogues were mixed with the CD solution. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of CDs did not changed significantly (red bar). All the results that the fabricated probe possessed good selectivity for benzidine and could be used for the specific recognition of benzidine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence mechanism for the detection of benzidine
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, fluorescence methods are established based on different mechanisms, and fluorescence quenching mechanism is one of the often-used mechanisms. Fluorescence quenching mechanisms usually include static quenching, dynamic quenching, energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer and IFE [56,57], etc.

As CDs were concerned, static quenching often occurred when a non-fluorescent ground-state complex appeared based on the interaction between CDs and the quencher. The existence of the ground-state complex would result in the change of CDs absorption spectrum [58]. As shown in Fig. 2A, in the presence of benzidine, the fluorescence of CD at about 303 nm was significantly quenched. In order to clarify the accurate quenching mechanism, a series of experiments were designed and performed. From Fig. 5 A, it can be seen that the sensing system does not have new different absorption peaks compared with the UV–Vis spectra of CD, benzidine and CD + benzidine, indicating that there was no new complex forming between benzidine and CDs. This result excluded the possibility of static quenching. In Fig. 5B, the absorption spectrum of benzidine effectively overlapped not only the excitation spectrum of CDs, but also the emission spectrum, which indicated that its quenching mechanism may be related to IFE or FRET [59,60]. In order to distinguish the two mechanisms, we measured the fluorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. 5C, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs + benzidine was 3.25 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was 3.28 ns. The fluctuation of less than 1 ns did not cause obvious changes. Generally, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs is significantly reduced based on FRET mechanism. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of the synthesized CDs was mainly attributed to IFE [61,62].

For assessing the feasibility of the practical application of CDs, four real water samples were collected from Beijing Jingmi diversion canal, Shahe, Anhui Nanhu Park and Shanghai Pearl River. The real water samples were pretreated by filtering through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to dislodge impurities, and stored in the refrigerator. The blank and spiked samples at 0.5, 1 and 5 μM of benzidine were measured with CDs. As shown in Table 2 , benzidine was not detected in the blank water samples. The recoveries of spiked samples were achieved in the range of 97.4%∼106% and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were less than 4.5%.

In this study, strong fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized using amoxicillin and sodium carbonate as precursors by a simple and economic hydrothermal method. As-obtained CDs were stable at high salt environment and earned good long-term stability with little change of the fluorescence intensity for three months. The CDs based probe can selectively and sensitively detect benzidine and provided a two-stage linear range of 0.2–2 and 2–16 μM and low LOD of 0.08 μM. The probe was validated with real samples, and convinced spiked recoveries were achieved in the range of 97.4%∼106%. These data demonstrated that the designed probe was simple, rapid, low cost, highly selective and sensitive, which provided an effective tool for the detection of benzidine, and possessed great application prospect in environmental and other fields. However, the fluorescent probe can only detect one pollutant specifically, which is not conducive to the analysis and detection of multiple pollutants in the environment at the same time. At present, the use of CDs-based fluorescent probes for simultaneous analysis and detection of multiple pollutants is rare. In the future, the development of CDs-based fluorescent probes in this direction is very promising and will promote the wide application of CDs.

Menghua Liu: Project administration, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Shuangying Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Zhi Li: Investigation, Software. Yanhui Li: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yue Zhang: Formal analysis. Jingwen Niu: Investigation, Software, Validation. Linchun Nie: Investigation, Software, Validation. Chunmao Chen: Investigation, Software, Validation. Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Screening of some banned aromatic amines in textile products from Indian bandhani and gamthi fabric and in human sweat using micellar liquid chromatography
